#### [Session 1](https://redmoneyevents.com/event/ifninvestorme2025/\#session-01)

**From Alternative to Mainstream: Successfully Positioning Shariah Compliant Investment Management for the Next Generation**

What trends are influencing Islamic investment management in terms of investment patterns, asset allocation and product development? Has Islamic asset management been positioned and promoted effectively, and what more could be done? Has increased demand for Shariah compliant investment products impacted global investment patterns? We ask an expert panel.

**Moderator:**

**Oliver Agha**, _Professional Associate, Outer Temple Chambers_

**Panelists:**

**Alisher Djumanov**, _Founder, AD Wealth_

**Fahd Chaudhry**, _Director, Client Coverage MENA, CIS and Turkey, S&P Dow Jones Indices_

**Hakan Ozyon**, _CEO, Hejaz Financial Services_

**Mehdi Popotte**, _Portfolio Manager, Arqaam Capital_

From Alternative to Mainstream: Successfully Positioning Shariah Compliant Investment Management for the Next Generation from IFN TV on Vimeo

#### [Session 2](https://redmoneyevents.com/event/ifninvestorme2025/\#session-02)

**Beyond the Drawing Board: Creativity and Innovation in Regional Islamic Investment and Wealth Management**

What innovation is being seen in Islamic investment and wealth management to develop new products, grow customer bases and build brands, and how is technology being deployed? What is the potential for Waqf investment funds and what has already been achieved in the region? How are Waqf investment funds structured and how will these funds contribute to sustainable development?

**Moderator:**

**Vineeta Tan**, _Director, REDmoney and Managing Editor of IFN Investor_

**Panelists:**

**Ebrahim Moolla**, _Head of Islamic Private Capital, Numeral_

**Dr Imran Lum**, _Head of Islamic Finance, Business & Private Bank, National Australia Bank_

**Max Garvey**, _Founder and CEO, Epicenter Labs_

**Thamer Al Shahry**, _Partner, SASLO_

#### [Session 3](https://redmoneyevents.com/event/ifninvestorme2025/\#session-03)

**The Role and Potential of Private Markets in Global Islamic Investment**

What are current market trends in private capital and what are the underlying factors driving growth? What opportunities are on offer for regional Shariah compliant private capital investment, and if we examine asset-backed credit, venture funds, private equity, real assets, and real estate sectors, where will we see the growth? Can private capital more effectively fund Shariah compliant startups and foster innovation across global markets?

**Moderator:**

**Alexander Armstrong**, _Director, Aditum Capital_

**Panelists:**

**Anita Wieja-Caruba**, _Associate Director, Policy and Legal, Dubai Financial Services Authority_

**Lo’ai Bataineh**, _CEO, Ominvest Capital DIFC_

**Mohammed Parekh**, _Managing Director, MRP Premier Group_

**Ruslan Akhmedzyanov**, _Head of Transaction Advisory, Ypsilon Capital (DIFC)_

**Walter Gontarek**, _CEO and Chairman, Channel Capital Advisors_

#### [Session 4](https://redmoneyevents.com/event/ifninvestorme2025/\#session-04)

**Power Panel: Battle of the Shariah Compliant Asset Classes**

Through a series of short, punchy presentations we examine the merits of various asset classes from an investor’s viewpoint. With each asset class presented by a recognized market practitioner, we offer a critical assessment of Islamic investment opportunities, from private capital, to Sukuk, to equities, to real estate. Which asset class will prevail?

**Moderator:**

**Vineeta Tan**, _Director, REDmoney and Managing Editor of IFN Investor_

**Panelists:**

**Jaber Al Noumani**, _CFO – Finance and Procurement, OQ Exploration and Production_

**Khalid Howladar**, _Chairman, MRHB.Network_

**Louis Hems**, _Commercial Director, Dubai Gold and Commodities Exchange_

#### [Session 6](https://redmoneyevents.com/event/ifninvestorme2025/\#session-06)

**Islamic Sustainable Finance: Unlocking the Power and Potential of Islamic Investment**

How much further can regional Shariah compliant asset owners and managers incorporate green and sustainable considerations into investment decisions, while at the same time encouraging investee companies to manage climate risk? What is a current assessment of regulatory frameworks and their influence on Shariah compliant investment decisions? Is sufficient regional Shariah compliant capital being channeled into energy transition, and are we maximizing the potential of green Sukuk?

**Moderator:**

**Sohail Zubairi**, _Green Islamic Finance Specialist_

**Panelists:**

**Faisal Ali**, _Senior Portfolio Manager, Azimut_

**Luma Saqqaf**, _Founder and CEO, Ajyal Sustainability_

**Mona Zeineldin**, _Managing Director, Emerging Capital Management Partners_

#### About The IFN Investor Middle East Forum

Again, we open the year with our excellent IFN Investor Middle East Forum. This forum will feature carefully curated topics – the most pertinent in the world of Islamic investment management today.

Held in one of the most attractive global destinations for Islamic investment, Dubai, IFN Investor Middle East Forum will host leading global experts and industry professionals to engage in high-level discussions on the opportunities, challenges and the future in Shariah investment, private equity and venture capital as well as sovereign wealth and family offices.

IFN Investor Middle East Forum, now in its second year, is held in conjunction with the recently launched IFN Investor, a news and data media platform focusing on global Islamic investments and asset management industry covering global, regional and local trends to enable institutions and retail investors and providers make data-driven informed decisions.

#### The Previous IFN Investor Middle East Forum

## IFN Investor Middle East Forum 2024

We are opening the year with an exciting new brand – IFN Investor Middle East Forum. This forum will feature carefully curated topics – the most pertinent in the world of Islamic investment management today.

Held in one of the most attractive global destinations for Islamic funds, Dubai, IFN Investor Middle East Forum will host leading global experts and industry professionals to engage in high-level discussions on the opportunities, challenges and the future in Shariah investment, private equity and venture capital as well as sovereign wealth and family offices.

IFN Investor Middle East Forum is held in conjunction with the launch of IFN Investor, a new media platform focusing on Islamic investments and asset management covering global, regional and local trends to enable institutions and retail investors and providers make data-driven informed decisions.

REDmoney _events_ designs, organizes and hosts industry-leading conferences, forums, roadshows, seminars and dialogues focusing on the Islamic financial markets across a global, regional and national level. Leveraging an exceptional network of the industry’s elite and supported by the expertise of the REDmoney publishing and seminars divisions, REDmoney Events are able to provide access to a unique array of influential speakers, panelists and participants from a cross-section of the global industry to debate the most significant issues affecting the Shariah compliant financial markets.

REDmoney _events_ are differentiated by their complimentary yet qualified attendance structure, ensuring the highest quality of both delegates and contributors. Comprehensive and cuttingedge, attendance ensures exclusive access to the leaders in their field across all sectors and markets.

The annual IFN Asia and IFN UK Forums are categorically recognized as their continent’s leading industry events since their inception in 2006 and 2007, respectively. The Dialogues series have also become an integral element of the REDmoney events series and have cemented their position as leading, must-attend, events.

In 2024, we welcome the IFN Investor Forums, which coincide with the new IFN Investor portal.

web.description: And with AI reshaping organizations, what does a winning culture look like in 2026? To answer this, we invited some of the world's leading CEOs and experts.
web.markdown:
[To main content](https://www.nbim.no/en/investments/annual-investment-conference/2026-investment-conference/#main-content)

## What's a winning culture?

Corporate culture is the only competitive advantage you can't copy – and the hardest to build. Can you measure it? Transform it? Scale it without losing it? And with AI reshaping organizations, what does a winning culture look like in 2026? To answer this, we invited some of the world’s leading CEOs and experts.

[Conference programme](https://www.nbim.no/en/investments/annual-investment-conference/2026-investment-conference/2026-investment-conference-programme/)

[Speakers](https://www.nbim.no/en/investments/annual-investment-conference/2026-investment-conference/2026-speakers/)

## Jamie Dimon – Chairman and Chief Executive Officer at JP Morgan Chase

Jamie Dimon is Chairman of the Board and Chief Executive Officer of JPMorgan Chase & Co., a global financial services firm with assets of $3.2 trillion and operations worldwide.

Dimon became CEO on January 1, 2006 and one year later also became Chairman of the Board. He was named President and Chief Operating Officer upon the company’s merger with Bank One Corporation on July 1, 2004. Dimon joined Bank One as Chairman and CEO in 2000.

Dimon began his career at American Express Company. Next, he served as Chief Financial Officer and then President at Commercial Credit, which made numerous acquisitions and divestitures, including acquiring Primerica Corporation in 1987 and The Travelers Corporation in 1993. Dimon served as President and Chief Operating Officer of Travelers from 1990 through 1998 while concurrently serving as Chief Operating Officer of its Smith Barney Inc. subsidiary before becoming co-Chairman and Co-CEO of the combined brokerage following the 1997 merger of Smith Barney and Salomon Brothers. In 1998, Dimon was named President of Citigroup Inc., the global financial services company formed by the combination of Travelers Group and Citicorp.

Dimon earned his bachelor’s degree from Tufts University and holds an MBA from Harvard Business School. He serves on the boards of directors of a number of non-profit institutions including the Business Roundtable, Bank Policy Institute and Harvard Business School. Additionally, he serves on the executive committee of the Partnership for New York City, and is a member of the Business Council, Financial Services Forum and Council on Foreign Relations.

## Kenneth C. Griffin – Founder and Chief Executive Officer of Citadel

Kenneth Griffin is the Founder and Chief Executive Officer of Citadel, one of the world's leading alternative investment firms managing over $60 billion in capital. He is also the Founder and Non-Executive Chairman of Citadel Securities, one of the world's largest market makers serving more than 1,600 institutional clients globally.

Ken founded Citadel in 1990, a year after graduating from Harvard, where he had begun investing from his dorm room in 1986. He built the firm on the belief that exceptional talent, advanced quantitative analytics, and powerful technology could unlock opportunities in global capital markets.

Through Griffin Catalyst, Ken has made transformative philanthropic contributions to education, health sciences, and cultural institutions.

Ken holds an A.B. with Honors in Economics from Harvard College.

[In Good Company: Kenneth C. Griffin](https://www.nbim.no/en/news-and-insights/podcast/2024/citadel-founder-and-ceo-investing-innovation-culture-and-politics/)

## Christel Heydemann – Chief Executive Officer at Orange

Christel Heydemann was appointed Chief Executive Officer of the Orange group on April 4, 2022.

She began her career in 1999 at Alcatel, where she held various management positions. In 2011, she was promoted to Director of Human Resources and Transformation and member of the Executive Committee.

Christel Heydemann joined Schneider Electric in 2014. In 2017, she became Executive Director France Operations of Schneider Electric and member of Schneider Electric’s Executive Committee. In 2021, she was named Director Europe Operations, a position she held until the beginning of 2022 when she became Chief Executive Officer of the Orange group, having served as a member of its Board of Directors since 2017.

She is a graduate of École Polytechnique and École Nationale des Ponts et Chaussées. Christel Heydemann is an Officer of the French Order of Merit and a Knight of the French Legion of Honor.

[In Good Company: Christel Heydemann](https://www.nbim.no/en/news-and-insights/podcast/2025/orange-ceo-telecommunications-future-network-investment-and-leadership/)

## David M. Rubenstein – Co-Founder and Co-Chairman of the Board of Carlyle

Mr. Rubenstein is Co-Founder and Co-Chairman of the Board of Carlyle, one of the world’s largest and most diversified global investment firms. Previously, Mr. Rubenstein served as Co-Chief Executive Officer of Carlyle.

Among other philanthropic endeavors, Mr. Rubenstein is Chairman of the Boards of the Council on Foreign Relations, the National Gallery of Art, the Economic Club of Washington, and the University of Chicago; a Trustee of Memorial Sloan-Kettering Cancer Center, the Institute for Advanced Study, the Brookings Institution, and the World Economic Forum; an Emeritus Trustee of Johns Hopkins Medicine; and a Director of Moderna, Inc. and the American Academy of Arts and Sciences.

Mr. Rubenstein is a magna cum laude graduate of Duke University, where he was elected Phi Beta Kappa. Following Duke, Mr. Rubenstein graduated from the University of Chicago Law School, where he was an editor of the Law Review.

[In Good Company: David M. Rubenstein](https://www.nbim.no/en/news-and-insights/podcast/2025/david-rubenstein-defining-great-investors-guiding-presidents-and-preserving-history/)

## James P. Gorman – Chairman Emeritus of Morgan Stanley

James P. Gorman is Chairman of The Walt Disney Co. and Senior Advisor of General  Atlantic. He is also Chairman Emeritus of Morgan Stanley.

Prior to becoming Chairman Emeritus in January 2025, Mr. Gorman served as Executive Chairman from January to December 2024, CEO from January 2010 until December 2023 and Chairman from January 2012 until December 2023. He joined the Firm in February 2006 and was named Co-President in December 2007.

Before he joined Morgan Stanley, Mr. Gorman held a succession of executive positions at Merrill Lynch, and prior to this was a senior partner of McKinsey & Co. He began his career as an attorney in Melbourne, Australia.

Separately, Mr. Gorman serves as a Director of the Council on Foreign Relations, Co-Chair of the Board of Overseers of the Columbia Business School and Trustee of Columbia University.

He formerly served as a Director of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, President of the Federal Advisory Council to the U.S. Federal Reserve Board, Co-Chairman of the Partnership for New York City, Chairman of the Board of the Securities Industry and Financial Markets Association (SIFMA), and Co-Chairman of the Business Committee of the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

Born in Australia, Mr. Gorman received BA and law degrees from the University of Melbourne and an MBA degree from Columbia University.

[In Good Company: James P. Gorman](https://www.nbim.no/en/news-and-insights/podcast/2023/morgan-stanley-chair-and-ceo-succession-culture-stress-and-leadership/)

## Karin Rådström – Chief Executive Officer of Daimler Truck

Karin Rådström has been Chief Executive Officer of Daimler Truck AG since October 2024. She previously served as a Member of the Board of Management responsible for Mercedes-Benz Trucks from February 2021 until her appointment as CEO. She brings extensive international experience in the commercial vehicles industry and a strong track record in building customer-focused sales organisations.

Prior to joining Daimler Truck, Karin spent 16 years at Scania, where she was most recently a member of the Executive Board responsible for sales and marketing. She held various managerial positions within Scania's sales and service organisation, including Head of the bus and coach business and leading the launch of the connected vehicle business.

Karin serves as a Board Member of Atlas Copco since April 2024. She holds a Master of Engineering in Industrial Management from the Royal Institute of Technology in Stockholm.

[In Good Company: Karin Rådström](https://www.nbim.no/en/news-and-insights/podcast/2025/daimler-truck-ceo-electric-transition-autonomous-vehicles-and-cultural-change/)

## Jesper Brodin – Former Chief Executive Officer of Ingka Group

Jesper Brodin served as Chief Executive Officer of Ingka Group, the largest IKEA retailer, from 2017 until November 2025, following a career with IKEA spanning more than 30 years. Before becoming CEO, Jesper was Managing Director at IKEA of Sweden, responsible for the development of the product range and supply chain of the IKEA brand.

Jesper began his IKEA career in Pakistan in 1995 as a purchase manager, later becoming Regional Purchase Manager for Southeast Asia. In 1999, he served as assistant to both IKEA founder Ingvar Kamprad and then-CEO Anders Dahlvig.

Jesper serves as Chair of The B Team and Co-Chair of the UN Global Compact, advocating for a future where purpose and profit go hand in hand. In 2024, he was named one of TIME's 100 most influential people.

Jesper holds a Master's degree in Industrial Engineering from Chalmers University of Technology, Sweden.

[In Good Company: Jesper Brodin](https://www.nbim.no/en/news-and-insights/podcast/2025/jesper-brodin-inside-ikea-democratic-design-and-sustainability-profits/)

## Stefano Domenicali – President and Chief Executive Officer of Formula 1

Stefano Domenicali has been President and Chief Executive Officer of Formula 1 since January 2021. He also serves as Co-Chairman of the F1 Commission and is a Member of the FIA World Motor Sport Council.

Stefano began his career at Ferrari in 1991, where he held positions of increasing responsibility over more than two decades. He served as Formula 1 Team Manager from 1998, Formula 1 Sports Director from 2004, and Team Principal of Scuderia Ferrari from 2008 to 2014, contributing to the team's many victories. He subsequently served as Vice President of New Business Initiatives at Audi AG from 2014, before becoming Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of Automobili Lamborghini from 2016 to 2020.

Stefano is a Member of the Board of Directors of Brunello Cucinelli and Ferretti Group. In 2023, he was appointed Cavaliere del Lavoro by the President of the Italian Republic.

Stefano holds a degree in economics and business from the University of Bologna.

[In Good Company: Stefano Domenicali](https://www.nbim.no/en/news-and-insights/podcast/2025/formula-1-ceo-sport-evolution-elite-drivers-and-full-speed-leadership/)

## Ajay Banga – President of the World Bank Group

Ajay Banga has been President of the World Bank Group since June 2023, leading the institution's transformation with a new vision to create a world free of poverty on a liveable planet.

Prior to joining the World Bank, Ajay was Vice Chairman at General Atlantic and, before that, President and CEO of Mastercard for 12 years, where he led a global workforce of nearly 24,000 and made financial inclusion a core part of the company's strategy. He founded the Mastercard Center for Inclusive Growth to advance equitable and sustainable economic opportunity. Earlier in his career, Ajay spent 13 years at Nestlé India and held senior leadership positions at Citigroup, including CEO of the Asia-Pacific region.

Ajay served as Chairman of the International Chamber of Commerce from 2020 to 2022 and is a co-founder of the Cyber Readiness Institute. He was awarded the Padma Shri by the President of India in 2016.

Ajay holds a Bachelor's degree in Economics from St. Stephen's College, University of Delhi, and an MBA from the Indian Institute of Management Ahmedabad.

[In Good Company: Ajay Banga](https://www.nbim.no/en/news-and-insights/podcast/2023/ajay-banga-president-of-world-bank-poverty-development-brics-and-india/)

## Robyn Grew – Chief Executive Officer at Man Group

Robyn Grew is the Chief Executive Officer of Man Group, a global alternative investment management firm with more than USD 200 billion assets under management that is listed on the London Stock Exchange. As CEO, she leads the firm’s Executive Committee and is an executive director on the Man Group Board.

Since joining Man Group in 2010 through its acquisition of GLG, Robyn has served as Group COO, Head of ESG, General Counsel, and most recently, President. Robyn led Man Group’s corporate reorganisation in 2019 and has also spearheaded the firm’s diversity programme.

Robyn has worked in the investment industry since 1994. Previously, she held senior global positions at investment banks in London, New York and Tokyo, including Barclays Capital and Lehman Brothers, as well as what is now the ICE Futures Europe exchange. She is a qualified barrister and member of the Standards Board for Alternative Investment (SBAI) Board of Trustees.

## Michael O’Leary – Group Chief Executive Officer at Ryanair

Michael O’Leary has served as a Director of Ryanair DAC since 1988 and a Director of Ryanair Holdings since 1996. Michael was appointed CEO of Ryanair in 1994 and Group CEO in April 2019, having previously served as CFO since 1988.

Under his leadership, Ryanair has grown to become Europe's largest airline group, pioneering the low-cost model that has transformed air travel across the continent. Michael holds a degree in Business and Economics from Trinity College Dublin.

[In Good Company: Michael O’Leary](https://www.nbim.no/en/news-and-insights/podcast/2022/guest-michael-oleary-ceo-of-ryanair/)

## Nandan M. Nilekani – Chairman and Co-Founder, Infosys and Founding Chairman UIDAI (Aadhaar)

Nandan Nilekani is the Co-Founder and Chairman of Infosys Technologies Limited. He was the Founding Chairman of the Unique Identification Authority of India (UIDAI) in the rank of a Cabinet Minister from 2009-2014. Nandan has co-founded and is the Chairman of EkStep Foundation, a not-for-profit effort to create a learner centric, technology-based platform to improve basic literacy and numeracy for millions of children. In January 2023, he was appointed as the co-chair of the “G20 Task Force on Digital Public Infrastructure for Economic Transformation, Financial Inclusion, and Development.”

Born in Bengaluru, Nilekani received his Bachelor’s degree from Indian Institute of Technology (IIT), Bombay. Among many awards and accolades, he was inducted as International Honorary Member of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences in 2019 and TIME Magazine has featured him among 100 Most Influential People in AI in 2024.

[In Good Company: Nandan M. Nilekani](https://www.nbim.no/en/news-and-insights/podcast/2023/guest-nandan-nilekani-founder-and-chairman-of-infosys/)

## Roula Khalaf – Editor of the Financial Times

Roula Khalaf has been Editor of the Financial Times since 2020. She previously served as Deputy Editor, overseeing a range of newsroom initiatives and award-winning editorial projects while leading a global network of over 100 foreign correspondents.

Before becoming Deputy Editor, Roula was the FT's Foreign Editor, overseeing operations in Europe, Africa and the Middle East. As Middle East Editor, she launched a Middle East edition and led coverage of the Arab Spring. She joined the FT in 1995 as North Africa correspondent, having previously been a staff writer for Forbes magazine in New York.

Roula is a Board Member of the Committee to Protect Journalists. She was named Foreign Commentator of the Year at the Editorial Intelligence Comment Awards in 2016.

# Cookie Policy

Last updated: March 24, 2026

This Cookie Policy applies to the Apex Invest event platform powered by Samaaro. It explains how cookies and similar technologies are used when you access and use the platform.

## 1\. Data controller

This platform is operated by Apex Invest. The event technology is provided by Samaaro. For the purposes of this Cookie Policy, Apex Invest is the data controller responsible for the processing of your personal data through cookies on this platform.

Samaaro acts as a data processor, processing data on behalf of Apex Invest in accordance with applicable data protection agreements.

## 2\. What are cookies?

Cookies are small text files stored on your device when you visit a website. They help websites function properly, improve user experience, and provide insights into usage. Some cookies are deleted when you close your browser (session cookies), while others remain on your device for a set period (persistent cookies).

## 3\. How we use cookies

We use cookies for the following purposes:

### Essential Cookies Always Active

These cookies are required for the platform to function correctly. They enable core features such as user authentication and session management. The legal basis for these cookies is **legitimate interest**, as they are strictly necessary for the operation of the platform.

| Cookie | Provider | Purpose | Duration |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| session\_id | Samaaro | Maintains secure user session after login or registration | Session |
| csrf\_token | Samaaro | Protects against cross-site request forgery attacks | Session |
| cookie\_consent | Samaaro | Stores your cookie consent preferences | 12 months |

These cookies cannot be disabled as they are necessary for platform operation.

### Analytics Cookies Optional

We use analytics tools such as Google Analytics to understand how users interact with the platform. The legal basis for these cookies is **consent**. They are only activated after you provide your consent through the cookie banner.

| Cookie | Provider | Purpose | Duration |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| \_ga | Google Analytics | Distinguishes unique users by assigning a randomly generated identifier | 2 years |
| \_ga\_\* | Google Analytics | Maintains session state across page requests | 2 years |
| \_gid | Google Analytics | Distinguishes unique users for session-level reporting | 24 hours |

These cookies collect information in an aggregated and pseudonymised manner, including page views, session duration, and engagement patterns. You can withdraw your consent at any time by clicking "Manage" on the cookie banner.

## 4\. International data transfers

Some of the cookies used on this platform, particularly those set by Google Analytics, may result in your data being transferred to and processed in countries outside of your jurisdiction, including the United States.

Where such transfers occur, they are supported by appropriate safeguards, including Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) approved by the European Commission or equivalent mechanisms, to ensure your data is protected to a standard consistent with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

## 5\. Cookie consent and control

A cookie consent banner is provided when you first visit the platform, allowing you to accept or manage your preferences for analytics cookies. You can change your preferences at any time by clicking "Manage" on the cookie banner at the bottom of the page.

You can also control and manage cookies through your browser settings. Most browsers allow you to block or delete cookies. Please note that disabling essential cookies may affect the functionality of the platform.

We do not use cookies for advertising, profiling, or cross-site tracking.

## 6\. Your rights

Depending on your jurisdiction, you may have the following rights in relation to the personal data collected through cookies:

- **Right of access** — request a copy of the personal data we hold about you
- **Right to rectification** — request correction of inaccurate or incomplete data
- **Right to erasure** — request deletion of your personal data, subject to legal obligations
- **Right to restrict processing** — request that we limit how we use your data
- **Right to data portability** — request your data in a structured, machine-readable format
- **Right to withdraw consent** — withdraw your consent for analytics cookies at any time via the cookie banner, without affecting the lawfulness of processing carried out prior to withdrawal
- **Right to lodge a complaint** — file a complaint with your local data protection authority if you believe your rights have been violated

To exercise any of these rights, please contact us using the details in Section 9 below.

## 7\. Data retention

Cookie data is retained for the duration specified in the cookie tables above. Analytics data processed by Google Analytics is retained in accordance with Google's data retention settings, which are configured to the minimum period necessary for our reporting purposes. You can delete cookies stored on your device at any time through your browser settings.

## 8\. Updates to this policy

We may update this Cookie Policy from time to time to reflect changes in technology, legal requirements, or platform functionality. Any material changes will be posted on this page with an updated "Last updated" date. We encourage you to review this policy periodically.

## 9\. Contact us

If you have any questions about this Cookie Policy, wish to exercise your data rights, or have concerns about how your data is being processed, please contact us:

**Apex Invest**

Event platform powered by Samaaro

Email: [success@samaaro.com](mailto:success@samaaro.com)

## 10\. Related policies

This Cookie Policy should be read in conjunction with our Privacy Policy, which provides further details on how we collect, use, store, and protect your personal data across the platform. If you have not already reviewed it, we encourage you to do so.

# [Global Overview: Capital in a Changing World | Global Investors' Symposium 2026](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ymJWo_3A8mA)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Milken Institute](https://www.youtube.com/@MilkenInstitute)
**Uploaded at**: 2026-03-23
**Published at**: 
**Length**: 44:13
**Views**: 8680
**Likes**: 175
**Category**: Nonprofits & Activism

## Description

```
Watch all sessions from Global Investors' Symposium 2026: https://milkeninstitute.org/events/global-investors-symposium-hong-kong-2026/program

The global macro picture at the end of 2025 was one of uncertainty, uneven growth, and pleasant surprises. Persistence and resilience were the name of the economic game, but through the headwinds emerged opportunities to redirect global capital, build new innovation hubs, engage with different partners, and redefine growth models. In this session, leading investors and executives will provide insights on where global markets may go in the year ahead, the policies that may impact business activities and capital flows, and strategies to stay ahead in today’s complex environment. 

Chery Kang
Correspondent and Anchor, "Squawk Box Asia," CNBC International

Aaron Costello
Head, Asia, Cambridge Associates

Christopher Ganis
Chief Investment Officer, Indonesia Investment Authority

Kevin Lu
Chairman, Asia, Partners Group

Hoi Tung
CEO and Chairman, Ping An Overseas Holdings
```

So, I think they becomes all very
competitive. I think viewing when you
invest in China, obviously, I think
these are two areas I think
you know, has a structural
benefit uh given the government support,
given the the the em- embedded at
competitive advantage, as well as, you
know, the human resources and talents
and pool that you can draw upon. I think
these are all very positive to me. And
certainly, I think you people should
think about these things, how to get
involved in these, exposed in these two
sectors. But there are many other, you
know, you know, China's so big. And
obviously, you know, there's obviously
there is a lot of idiosyncratic, you
know, investment opportunities, you
know, available. But uh these are two I
think structurally definitely uh very
very positive uh trends of people
shouldn't miss to invest, I suppose.
Anybody who wants to react to what Hoi
just said or
disagree?
I would just add, again, China, back to
this renewables thing. I mean, actually
the currency has held up well. The
markets, depending on the day, have held
up relatively well because they've made
these investments and they actually have
reduced their reliance on on oil. You
know, they roll out electric cars. So,
again, they've kind of paved the the
road map there. Investing in renewables,
yes, it's good for the environment, but
it's a national security issue. And so,
I think that's kind of people going to
take that and could take it take that
kind of road map and take it forward. I
would also say that yeah, like last year
on this stage, I think we were talking
about how unloved China was and the
skepticism that foreign investors have
to it. Is that changing? It's So, I
would say from my own experience,
um I've had more conversations with
clients about whether we should invest
in China, should we come visit China,
arranging trips to get on the ground.
Um there's been a thaw, particularly
from Europeans. Uh the Americans are
slowly warming up. I think a lot of it
just has to do with um
you know, it's To me, it's very clear
going back to October, the Trump
administration wants to strike a deal
with China. I think he's actually now
even more desperate to strike a deal
with China. He needs some sort of good
news. Um and so yes, they've delayed the
meeting. I don't think that means the
meeting is off. I think they we're going
to try to deal with this uh the Iran
situation, and then I think he he this
this notion of going to China, showing a
deal, showing a thaw, I think that's
still there. And so, I think the
geopolitical risk premium investing in
China actually has shrunk. And I think,
you know, if China can deliver
Ultimately, can China deliver the
earnings growth and returns, then the
investors will come back. Mhm. And I
think it's interesting how more people
want to set up visits to China. Do you
think it comes from maybe the
realization that they don't really
understand the China sitting all the way
in the US or all the way in Europe? Do
you think that's been happening for too
long?
>> Yeah, I mean I think COVID derailed
everything. So, pre-COVID, you know, our
clients and colleagues traveling to
China We have an office in Beijing, like
traveling all the time, lots of
investment. COVID hit, you couldn't
travel for practical reasons, and then
yes, the Chinese economy slowed, Chinese
investments underperformed, the
geopolitical pushback got got too too
much, and so people started visiting
India and Southeast Asia and and Japan
to some extent. And I think now that the
Chinese asset classes have come back,
that's kind of proof in the pudding. And
then yes, there's been this thaw at
least in US-China uh relations. And just
the reality that maybe because they
haven't been in four or five years, they
actually don't know what's going on. And
yes, the AI themes and the biotech
themes where China is leading, I mean
that that's got people's attention. And
so, I think that you're seeing the
thawing of that, and I think that will
continue. Mhm. And Chris, I I will get
to the Indonesia case, but I want to
just uh pick your brain, Kevin, first.
In terms of in the spirit of looking for
new opportunities in areas that didn't
run so much in valuations, how are you
thinking about uh things um
at Partners Group? Yeah, maybe I will I
will start by adding on the China
topics, and we'll we'll naturally come
to your your your your question as well,
right? Um if you look at China, a couple
of very quick points. One is that
whether an investor should invest in
China or not to some extent depending on
your exposure, right? Depending on your
asset class, on your exposure. If you're
overexposed, you want to think
carefully. If you are not, you should
look at China carefully. There's a
clearly a uh
a pivot the last 12-15 months from
institutional investor through Hong
Kong, for example, right? Picking up
that interest, which which which sort of
was low after COVID. Um but there two
other points on China I have very high
conviction about, which I will use less
conditional statement. One is the the
Chinese capital, the greater China
capital, their in the their level of
interest in going to Europe, going to
ASEAN is very very clear. That that that
we pick up a lot of conversation, and I
think that's historic level in terms of
the the both the sovereign and and
private capital wanting to be more
connected to to markets in Europe,
markets in the rest of Asia. And and
secondly, and related to that, in terms
of the between our portfolio companies,
like Chinese portfolio companies, other
Asian portfolio companies, European,
even US portfolio companies, how that
linkage is I think I think I think Hoy
mentioned about as well, right? The
value creation between those companies,
that is very clear, right? Regardless of
whether necessarily you invest into
China today, right? Um in terms of
opportunities, I would I would add Maybe
maybe again, I will respond to something
you about You and Chris both mentioned
about signals and noises, right? So, I I
would expand that slightly. I think I
think the world has three buckets in my
mind. Um the first bucket are big shifts
that are structural. There are many of
them that are today. I don't even name
them, right? The role of US dollar, the
role of US as the security provider. The
second category are the changes that are
short time, that that that come and go,
right? The third bucket, to me, is the
most interesting, is things that are
very predictable, almost very boring,
right? And again, if you go back to
China, and um I think Kevin Sneader told
us earlier that Goldman has raised their
their GDP growth for China, um big news,
you know, from 4.8 to 4.7.
Okay? That's very predictable. Okay? So,
China in the way you look at China every
year, the round of two sessions in
March, make those policy discussions,
and and the central government
announcement in January, the first one
is always about agriculture, right? So,
these are very very predictable things
that that we investors could rely on.
So, for us in
investing in the private markets, there
are a number of pockets that I think
it's very predictably relevant for
sort of investors. Infrastructure, for
example, right? Regardless of how the
world changes, many countries need more
infrastructure investment. That create
opportunity AI or not, right? It is very
very true. And and and and another very
technical point is, for example, I mean,
many of you are private market
investors. If you look at, for example,
the secondaries investments in private
markets, right? It's almost a numerical
calculation how big that market is. It
depending on 5, 8, 10 years ago, how
much fund raised, right? So, so so last
year, for example, in this very boring
topic, that the the whole the whole
world transacted 286 billion of private
equity secondary investments. 10 years
ago, that's about between 30 and 40
billion, right? You could predict that.
And it's very So, these are areas in
spite of all the big changes,
geopolitics, or whatever philosophical
statements that people make, we as as a
global investor can predictably look at
area where we could deploy our capital
with good return. Thank you, Kevin, for
that. And uh I want to
get to the case of Indonesia, Chris. And
I INA was established to basically bring
in foreign as well as the domestic
capital into the private sector of
Indonesia. That's what your mandate now.
I wonder some of the
challenging narratives that that's
playing out in Indonesia's governance,
the fiscal policy, and so on. Is that
making some capital flows hesitate to
actually come to Indonesia at the
moment?
Yeah, I mean, I don't I don't think we
should dismiss some of the question
raised by foreign investors, uh
especially as situations are unfolding,
right? I think we have to accept the
fact that Indonesia is still uh net oil
importer. Yeah. Right? That could create
uh significant change on the fiscal
number if oil continues to hover at
elevated level, call it like $95 a
barrel, right? But I think what we have
to keep in mind, try to take a step
back,
is understand that,
you know, on an overall basis, Indonesia
is uh net energy exporter. Mhm. Right?
So, how do we balance out? How does the
government balance out in the fiscal and
on the monetary side as well uh in
ensuring that
this negative net oil importer can be
negated by the net energy exporter and
net commodity exporter will be much more
critical, right? So, I think that's part
of the reason uh why we're here and try
to ensure that these communication
channel continue to be uh open to uh
all of our investors and our key
partners.
Uh I think the perception on the public
sector uh versus the private sector, I
think the private sector that takes a
much longer view
uh continue to have a stable and healthy
interest of investing in Indonesia.
I think the
the narrative that has continued to go
on uh
especially with this crisis, going back
to that people are trying to diversify
their supply chain, people are trying to
get as good close customer base, uh and
people are trying to get close to the
raw material.
I think, Kevin, you brought up about uh
the capex expansion for infrastructure,
right? That is what we're trying to uh
catalyze as well, because the difficulty
on that is you have to acknowledge that
central bankers around the world
are probably in a very tough place
for the last I can't remember, maybe
like
10, 15 years, right? But they they will
get through this.
Uh so, understanding that
the long-term infra asset will still be
required, how do we navigate the the mid
the short- and the mid-term challenges
as relates to fiscal and monetary uh
will be critical. Um
if you want to look at it holistically,
the
crisis that we're facing right now as
relates to energy is not
it's relatively an Asian crisis as well,
right? And on an overall basis, we are
still
uh an oil importer.
And how do we navigate that together
holistically uh will be uh will be very
critical. Mhm. Do you think
the Iran war is impactful enough to
have sovereigns to think about this oil
import dependence
longer term? Or do you think this is
just, "Okay, one tweet, and then we move
on to the next market concern"?
Yeah, I mean, this is the part where I
can't really quite reconcile in my head,
right? If we become the student of
history,
the the last oil crisis in the last, I
don't know, two, three, four decades,
it takes about 5 years to be able to get
to a normal level again in terms of the
supply.
But at the same time, you're also having
countries
to aggressively increase their reserve,
right? So, balancing that out,
it will create a structural issue on the
supply, right? But structural issue
doesn't always
necessarily bad, right? If you I think
Aaron to bring on your point that the US
is
net oil exporter, we have to keep going
back to that it create it was first
created as a structural issue
that led to the shell boom in 2010 or
something, right? As China demand for
oil spiked up
in the in the rise, right? So, how do we
react to that and how do we monetize
that
is much more important. And obviously,
I'm saying this
with all the the respect on everything
that is happening in the Middle East.
Obviously, it's a it's a real tragedy,
not just a monetary opportunity.
Time flies.
I need to get to the AI angle really
quickly and the whole private credit
concerns here. So, I think so, for me
personally, my New Year's resolution
was, okay, like you got to really get
this AI thing, you know, understood.
Like I I need to use AI more, don't get
behind, and I'm already behind, and now
the market is catching up to the
reality. It's all perspective concerns,
Aaron, but it it could be real. Do you
think
what happens, do you think, in that
digestion phase that people trying to
figure out what AI can do and how
disruptive it will be? Walk us through
your scenario in terms of how the market
would react to all this.
That's a very easy question. No.
Look, I mean, I I think taking a step
back, again, kind of
big picture, not short-term.
If AI truly achieves what people think
it's going to achieve, it's it's a it's
a tide that should lift
all boats, not just seven companies in
the US, right? Individual companies that
that implement AI will reap the benefit,
just like when the internet was
unveiled. You know, the kind of the
their their their stock prices went up
in the '90s, and then they kind of did
nothing in the 2000s, whereas all the
other companies benefited and there were
new companies created. So, I think
something similar is going to play out.
So, the markets now are very
concentrated in this handful of AI
infrastructure companies or whatever you
want to call them.
And they're spending more, their free
cash flow is falling,
higher energy prices and input costs can
actually derail the data center roll out
and then some of all of these things or
they slow it.
And so, again, like we want to be
diversified, not overly concentrated.
And if you think longer term, it's
companies that are enablers or the kind
of the users of AI will benefit, right?
And then you're going to have some
disruption. And so, I think the other
trend of the past few years, and some of
the some of the managers in the in the
audience may agree, it's been
pretty rough for active managers because
you've just had a handful of stocks
going up and up and up and the
benchmarks become more and more
concentrated. Hard to beat that. But if
those companies start to plateau in
terms of growth and performance and
there's all this disruption from AI,
well, now it can be more of a stock
picker's
market. We actually think hedge funds
are coming back to to kind of benefit
from that dispersion across sectors.
Also, just again, equity valuations have
gone a little bit crazy. We want to be
more diversified, kind of less
correlated strategies in in general. So,
I think the disruption from AI is real,
but it doesn't
imply that handful of companies have
been that have been benefiting the most
of the past two or three years are going
to continue to do so. And so, we need to
look more broadly. And just to piggyback
on him, right now we're all focused on
oil, but the reality is if we're really
going to be building armies of robots
and stuff, that's that's metals, right?
That that's that's other things that
actually Indonesia kind of has or some
of some other countries in the world
have. So, you know, again, we're all
fixated on oil, but actually the future
of AI is rare earths and metals and and
all of that stuff. So, again, we got to
take a longer term view here and and
figure out where those things are. And
along the way, software related
investments are being questioned here,
and it's not everyone. And
maybe it's just really some of the
smaller guys
having sort of relaxed underwriting
rules or practices.
But could that lead to a bigger problem
is my question. And I wonder if that is
a factor that can actually delay some of
the AI related progress or developments.
I know, Houy, that you think that
it's okay. Like we don't have to worry
too much about it
as long as, you know, things are done
right.
And then I pushed back on that in
pre-discussion. Not everyone is doing
the right thing in terms of
underwriting.
What do you have to say about that?
I would just like to the SAS. I think
the overall sector valuation has come
has come down quite quite quite
dramatically. I have to admit it because
that because the valuation run up very
high during the COVID period. Everybody
going to online. So, that's why. And
then came down.
Has been has been coming down, but
certainly the impact AI had sort of
really it had further exacerbated the
the decline, right? So, that's a
reality.
And obviously, whether or not the the
valuation will come up again or will
stay at this very low level or even have
a further room to go down, I I I can't
predict. But the problem is when you
look at these companies that have to do
underwriting, you have to look at the
fundamentals of each of the SAS
companies. There's some of the company
with with very you know, high entry
barriers, even but also at the same
time, the company itself could could
embrace AI, using AI to help them to
rewrite re re-engineer the workflows,
the processes, making more competitive.
So, that's it's not really static, I
suppose. But the old old in company will
die and the the AI natives will take
over the entire world. I don't believe
by that story, but obviously, I think
this is mutual. The company that could
fully embrace and take the opportunity,
improve the workflow through AI, that
will definitely survive. And they have
certainly also those big company or the
good these companies has quite a,
you know, the
very high mode of the with the
the system of record and also
some of the results requires to be very
as probably deterministic, very precise
answers. The AI couldn't give you that.
So, the AI is basically probabilistic
outcome. So, so these are with all that,
I think, you know, this definitely is
going to be combination of
the some of the incumbents will change
and obviously, some of the wouldn't
change or couldn't change, they will
will die. And then then the I think
that's probably the combination of the
both in the end.
Having said that, I'm seeing you you you
you question relates to the private
credit, I would suppose. I would say the
credit is all about, you know,
diversification. You can't have a one
single very huge concentration in your
portfolio. If you do that well, I think
you
even though the SAS has some impact on
overall portfolio, but that has I think
that that probably is going to be
manageable. And I think the nervousness
comes from the fact that private market
is private. So, not everyone is sort of
across the information and really
understand what's going on. And on on
that note, I want to sort of open up the
floor for maybe Aaron and Kevin cuz I
feel like Aaron can um
ask Kevin about the idea of the whole
the mantra of democratization of a
private markets and how that can be a
source of nervousness of playing out.
Well, I I don't want to be set up. It's
not me versus Kevin. I think there's
I think there's two things here. I think
all the headlines in private credit are
in the stress in the private credit that
we're seeing is these funds that were
marketed to quote unquote retail or high
net worth investors. And the issue is
not private credit as an asset class.
The issue here is the mismatch in
liquidity, saying this is a, you know,
we semi-liquid or you can get your money
back, when in reality, it's a private
asset class and the whole reason there's
an extra return is because it's illiquid
and locked up. So, I think the cardinal
sin here isn't
private credit or private markets in
general, it's trying to make them
liquid, which to me kind of defeats the
whole purpose of of the private markets.
And so, I also think here there's a
manager, maybe to Houy's comment,
there's a manager selection issue here
as well or vehicle selection issue where
some of these things aren't weren't as
diversified, were more lax. There's
plenty of opportunities in private
credit. And in fact, the distress in
some of these guys is going to create
opportunities for other funds that can
pivot into that. And so, I think that's
what investors should be leaning into.
So, it's not so much, you know, it's
just some of these vehicles were missold
and misstructured from the beginning.
And I think institutional investors
should just avoid those.
But then I'll turn it over to Kevin to
defend himself. Sure. Yeah, Sherry, you
you mentioned about the the uh the the
private information rifle for our
industry, which is which is absolutely
true, right? I was having the chat with
a couple of hedge fund friends
yesterday. We talked about two
differences between their industry and
ours. One is that they love volatility,
right? Whenever there's volatility, they
they can trade on that. We we private
managers want the stability. We want to
be able to sell and buy with certain
things. So, that's one thing we which we
work against us, but
my hedge fund friends cannot operate
with private information, and we can. We
can legally do so, right? So, that's how
that's the essential source of our
return for our LPs. That's why I just
point out that first, right? And then on
the private credit side, maybe two quick
points. One is that private credit
particularly is a very very broad
definition, right? There's so many
different forms of private lending
that's going on within this banner of
private credit. People seem to follow
the headlines and there's a problem.
People need to look specifically which
bucket of that is problem, which is not.
The majority of this space is not
problematic in my view, right? And my
final point is is what Aaron mentioned.
Um the reason this this problem today
people get so much media attention to
large extent has to do with the
offerings by GPs to especially
individual and retail investor over the
last 5 years, right? So, you use the
word the democratization of private
markets, and we that's definitely an
industry trend that we're broadening the
access from institutions to individual,
and in that process different GP has
different ways of working through the
plumbing, so to speak, right? So, it's
not only the content of private credit
investment, but also the plumbing and
how do you sell that to your
individuals, right? So, for Partners
Group, we are actually we started to to
to sell to individual 25 years ago. Our
first private evergreen so-called
product was 2001, right? So, we have
done the plumbing for 25 years, and we
still run the largest evergreen fund,
which is $16 billion or USPE. Plus, we
have done all the plumbing. So, if you
done the plumbing right, and your
content is largely robust, then you have
a much fewer chance to get into the
problem today, right? So, today in short
for private credit is there's a a few
segments of private credit that are
problematic. A bigger problem is I think
some of our friendly peers probably have
structured in a way that is a little
more too adventurous from a liquidity
perspective. Don't get too adventurous,
I guess. So, Chris, your final thoughts
on private credit related concerns.
Yeah, I mean, I think I could all the
gentlemen to my left and right that
from where I'm sitting private the
events that are transpiring in the
private credit space is more of a
uh increasing the discipline, not
exactly the destruction or the demise of
private credit. I mean, I think
specifically from Asia and Indonesia
point of view,
a couple of
points that I'd like to raise. One is
let's start with the most important
first
that I'm very grateful that the event
that's happening in private credit is
not doesn't have a contagious effect uh
in the way that we have seen in global
financial crisis. There's no systematic
effect that I I could really think of,
right? And even when we think about some
of the headline numbers, you know,
uh the
the the default rate back in the '08 '09
versus where it is now for the higher
bonds,
uh it's night and day, right? We're
talking about 9 10% in '08 '09, I mean,
'07 '08, and then now it's about 2 3%,
and we're still having a
a public market that's still
functioning.
Uh the second part that is also
important from an Asia point of view,
these private assets are here to
complement the public market. It's not
so much to replace the traditional
capital providers like in the developed
market like the banks or the bond
market, right? So, there's still a
natural need for that.
And I think this is a good step, a good
phase for the asset class
to understand the the the advantage it
brings, but also acknowledge
the the limitation of this private
class, right? That, you know, you can't
really simply have a very loose
uh
redemption. Mhm.
All right, Chris. Uh thank you so much
for that, and um I'm going to wrap it up
there. I tried to uh really track where
the capital is going in this uh changing
world. I thank you so much uh for having
me for this uh panel, important
conversation, and of course the forum
Milken as well. Thanks everyone for
listening in, and uh please enjoy rest
of the event.
Thank you.

[Register Now](https://opalgroup.net/finance/global-investment-conference-2026/#register)

# Mastering the Complexity of the Global Investment Landscape

Opal Group is honored to announce our **2026 Global Investment Conference**. Please join us and connect with industry leaders, investment professionals, and thought innovators at this year’s event, designed to guide you through the ever-evolving global investment landscape. Attendees will include family offices, ultra-high-net-worth investors, and impactful advisors; all looking for interesting networking opportunities.

**Some of the topics that will be addressed are:**

Navigate shifting markets with confidence.

Leverage emerging technologies to stay ahead.

Address geopolitical complexities impacting investments.

Don’t miss this opportunity to gain valuable insights, network with top professionals, and shape your approach to a dynamic global economy.

\*Subject to Opal Group’s verification and approval. Only individuals who are solely investing on their own behalf can be verified for a complimentary pass. Solution Providers or Fund Providers to third parties will not be able to receive complimentary access. As investment portfolios/strategies are constantly changing, we reserve the right to re-verify past delegates for future complimentary passes.

![GC26 Enhanced Hero_2](https://milkeninstitute.org/sites/default/files/styles/hero_2400_x_scale/public/2026-02/GC26%20Enhanced%20Hero_2%20%5B1920x1080%5D%20260204.jpg?itok=EfuqTRvv)

3 – 6 May 2026

# Global Conference 2026

Leading in a New Era

The **Milken Institute Global Conference** is the Institute’s flagship annual convening, bringing together leaders across health, finance, business, technology, philanthropy, and public policy. The 2026 conference focused on translating recent disruption and innovation into practical solutions for a more sustainable, equitable, and resilient future.

It was a unique experience in which individuals with the capital and influence to change the world connected with those whose expertise and creativity were reinventing health, finance, business, technology, philanthropy, industry, and society.

The **29th annual Global Conference** convened our diverse and forward-thinking community to tackle these urgent issues and seize opportunities to collectively shape a more sustainable, equitable, and resilient future.

[Download the Global Conference 2026 Fact Sheet](https://milkeninstitute.org/sites/default/files/2026-03/GlobalConference2026FactSheet.pdf)

Event Details

3 – 6 May 2026

Event Address

The Beverly Hilton & Waldorf Astoria Beverly Hills

Los Angeles

**Upcoming Global Conference Dates:**

- 2–5 May 2027
- 30 April – 3 May 2028
- 29 April – 2 May 2029
- 28 April – 1 May 2030

[**Sponsor Opportunities**](https://milkeninstitute.org/global-thought-leadership-sponsorship-opportunities)

## By the Numbers

## Solving Challenges

From economic mobility and workforce development to health, technology, energy, and global markets, the Global Conference convenes leaders to advance practical solutions across business, policy, and society.

![Panelist speaks while seated onstage at the Milken Institute Global Conference, gesturing during a discussion against a blue conference backdrop.](https://milkeninstitute.org/sites/default/files/styles/spotlight_665_x_510/public/2026-01/Solving-Challenges-GC26.jpg?itok=zIYyLbE4)

## Recent Milken Institute Speakers

Recent Milken Institute speakers have included global leaders such as Bill Clinton, Kristalina Georgieva, Jensen Huang, Mary Barra, and Queen Rania Al Abdullah, alongside influential voices from business, government, technology, and society.

[View Speakers](https://milkeninstitute.org/events/global-conference-2026/recent-milken-institute-speakers)

![Panelists sit onstage at the Milken Institute Global Conference, engaged in a moderated discussion, with one speaker addressing the audience as others listen.](https://milkeninstitute.org/sites/default/files/styles/spotlight_665_x_510/public/2026-01/Recent-Speakers-GC26.jpg?itok=a6_HazG6)

## Recommend a Global Conference Speaker

Milken Global Conferences feature the foremost experts and thought leaders on stage, and we welcome speaker recommendations for our events.

[Recommend Speaker](https://airtable.com/shrYOCfpB2E8P7SZV)

## Past Global Conferences

Politico

## Morning Money

POLITICO joined the Milken Institute to bring to life the 29th Annual Milken Institute Global Conference in a special edition of the “Morning Money” newsletter — covering the conversations shaping global markets, financial security, technology, health innovation, and international cooperation — with sharp reporting and insider insight on what matters for business, policy, and investors.

[Subscribe](https://www.politico.com/milkenglobal)

## FAQs

What is the Milken Institute Global Conference?

The Global Conference is the Milken Institute’s flagship annual convening, bringing together leaders from across sectors to address major economic, social, and technological challenges.

Who attends the Global Conference?

Attendees include global business leaders, investors, policymakers, philanthropists, researchers, nonprofit leaders, and innovators working across health, finance, technology, and social impact.

What is the focus of the 2026 Global Conference?

The 2026 conference focuses on translating recent disruption and innovation into practical solutions that support a more sustainable, equitable, and resilient future.

When and where is the 2026 Global Conference held?

Global Conference 2026 takes place May 3–6, 2026 in Beverly Hills, California (Los Angeles area), at [The Beverly Hilton](https://www.hilton.com/en/hotels/laxbhhh-the-beverly-hilton/) and the [Waldorf Astoria Beverly Hills](https://waldorfastoriabeverlyhills.com/).

How can organizations or individuals get involved?

Organizations and individuals can get involved by [attending the event](https://web.cvent.com/event/4a15598a-1c64-46e1-a374-8a2e76c25bfa/summary?RefId=Welcome%20to%20GC26&_gl=1*1iclxdd*_gcl_au*MTEyMTk0MzkwLjE3NjQ3NzkwODY.*_ga*MTk3NTQzOTk1LjE3NTY0MjI5Njg.*_ga_91PT67CNTD*czE3NzA4Mjg2MjIkbzQwMyRnMSR0MTc3MDgyOTEyMiRqNjAkbDAkaDA.), [becoming a sponsor](https://milkeninstitute.org/node/19074/), or [recommending a speaker](https://airtable.com/appX26xwm2OLDlh1L/shrYOCfpB2E8P7SZV).

# ESG & Sustainability Events in 2026: Important dates for your calendar

Event Date:

[![linkedIn logo](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65afed02803899625a2499bf/666b2b4b15235d8c2365972c_keyesg-ico-01.svg)](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https://www.keyesg.com/article/esg-events-important-dates-for-your-calendar&title=The%20top%20ESG%20and%20sustainability%20events%20in%202026%20|%20KEY%20ESG)[![X Logo](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65afed02803899625a2499bf/666b2b4bac7f93f1d48391a2_keyesg-ico-02.svg)](https://twitter.com/share?url=https://www.keyesg.com/article/esg-events-important-dates-for-your-calendar)

![EU ESG regulations, compliance, and regulatory oversight in sustainable finance](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65afed02803899625a2499c4/6932d1dcbc800466e2320022_%5BKEYE%5D%20AI%20ESG%20How%20KEY%20ESG%20is%20Streamlining%20Data%20Collection%20and%20Reporting.png)

ESG regulation and reporting expectations continue to accelerate, and 2026 is set to be a defining year for organisations working to strengthen their sustainability performance. Staying on top of key industry events is an effective way to understand evolving standards, learn from peers, and build more resilient reporting processes.

At KEY ESG, our team of experts tracks global regulatory developments and contributes to the wider sustainability conversation through webinars, workshops, and partner-led sessions. This calendar brings together the most relevant ESG and sustainability events for 2026 to help you plan ahead and stay informed.

Explore the list below and let us know if there are additional events we should feature. New dates will be added as they’re confirmed, so check back regularly for updates.

## **January & February**

### **Date:** January 13–15, 2026

**Event:** World Future Energy Summit

**Location:** Abu Dhabi, UAE

**Who’s it for:** Energy leaders, policymakers, sustainability executives and climate-tech innovators.

**Description**: A global forum on clean energy innovation, climate solutions and large-scale sustainability transitions.

**Find more info:** [https://www.worldfutureenergysummit.com/en-gb.html](https://www.worldfutureenergysummit.com/en-gb.html)

### Date: January 19–23, 2026

**Event:** World Economic Forum Annual Meeting

**Location:** Davos-Klosters, Switzerland

**Who’s it for:** World leaders from government, business, academia and civil society.

**Description:** A flagship gathering addressing global risks, economic transformation and sustainability challenges.

**Find more info:** [https://www.weforum.org/meetings/world-economic-forum-annual-meeting-2026/](https://www.weforum.org/meetings/world-economic-forum-annual-meeting-2026/)

### Date: January 26–28, 2026

**Event:** Cleantech Forum North America

**Location:** San Diego, CA, USA

**Who’s it for:** Climate-tech innovators, investors and corporate sustainability teams.

**Description:** A forum showcasing emerging climate technologies, investment trends and clean-industry innovation.

**Find more info:** [https://cleantech.com/event/cleantech-forum-na/agenda/](https://cleantech.com/event/cleantech-forum-na/agenda/)

### Date: January 27, 2026

**Event:** Real Deals Sustainable Investment Forum

**Location:** London, UK

**Who’s it for:** Private markets professionals, ESG leads and institutional investors.

**Description:** A forum examining ESG integration, disclosure expectations and sustainable value creation in private markets.

**Find more info:** [https://rdsustainableinvestmentforum.com/live/en/page/home](https://rdsustainableinvestmentforum.com/live/en/page/home)

### Date: February 12, 2026

**Event:** Private Equity Wire European Summit

**Location:** London, UK

**Who’s it for:** Private equity executives, fund managers and ESG practitioners.

**Description:** Discussions on ESG regulation, due diligence, reporting and risk management in European private equity.

**Find more info:** [https://events.privateequitywire.co.uk/private-equity-wire-eu-summit-26](https://events.privateequitywire.co.uk/private-equity-wire-eu-summit-26)

### Date: February 17–19, 2026

**Event:** GreenBiz 26

Last updated:

January 16, 2026

ESG regulation and reporting expectations continue to accelerate, and 2026 is set to be a defining year for organisations working to strengthen their sustainability performance. Staying on top of key industry events is an effective way to understand evolving standards, learn from peers, and build more resilient reporting processes.

At KEY ESG, our team of experts tracks global regulatory developments and contributes to the wider sustainability conversation through webinars, workshops, and partner-led sessions. This calendar brings together the most relevant ESG and sustainability events for 2026 to help you plan ahead and stay informed.

Explore the list below and let us know if there are additional events we should feature. New dates will be added as they’re confirmed, so check back regularly for updates.
